notes: Two-tier application model dominant in early 1990s; Aberdeen argues this should be evolved rather than abandoned.
Described as '1990s client for the masses'; server-centric execution model.
Aberdeen notes Java is limited by language/class library constraints for complex OLTP in 1996; prediction was accurate for the era.
Aberdeen notes suppliers moving away from CGI toward web-server-to-application-server partitioning; confirmed by Apache/IIS app servers.
Cited as best suited for client/server not browser model; confirmed — complex OLAP stayed thick-client until modern SaaS BI.
Complex OLTP cited as unsuitable for browser/web-server in 1996; still mostly server-side today.
Aberdeen predicts EDI will move to Internet (TCP/IP) over next 5 years from 1996; confirmed — AS2/AS4 and API-based EDI replaced traditional VAN-based EDI.
Aberdeen discusses intranet as ideal for pre-defined database queries and HR applications.
Aberdeen advocates for single Web-enabled client/server tool strategy rather than separate Internet development tools.
Aberdeen notes trend away from CGI toward app-server partitioning; this became the dominant n-tier architecture (WebSphere, IIS, Tomcat).
